title: Data-structure choice

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Data-structure choice; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Data-structure choice
sources.claim: Data-structure choice follows required operations and invariants: order, duplicates, positional access, key lookup, insertion, removal, traversal, and memory constraints.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Data-structure choice follows required operations and invariants: order, duplicates, positional access, key lookup, insertion, removal, traversal, and memory constraints.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A playback queue needs stable order and duplicate songs, so a sequence fits better than a set that removes duplicates. Your goal is to defend a data-structure choice and identify a reasonable alternative's trade-off. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iteration; Values and types; Records and entities; Sets and maps. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Data-structure choice follows required operations and invariants: order, duplicates, positional access, key lookup, insertion, removal, traversal, and memory constraints. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Big-O labels alone omit constant costs, scale, mutation patterns, library guarantees, concurrency, persistence, and readability for the actual workload. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: One structure, such as a list, is always best because it can represent every small dataset somehow.

includes: Compare data structures using order, duplicates, lookup, update and traversal needs.
Defend a data-structure choice and identify a reasonable alternative's trade-off.
excludes: Big-O labels alone omit constant costs, scale, mutation patterns, library guarantees, concurrency, persistence, and readability for the actual workload.

outcomes: Defend a data-structure choice and identify a reasonable alternative's trade-off.

examples.title: Data-structure choice: direct case
examples.situation: A playback queue needs stable order and duplicate songs, so a sequence fits better than a set that removes duplicates.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A playback queue needs stable order and duplicate songs, so a sequence fits better than a set that removes duplicates.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: List the dominant operations and invariants first, compare at least one alternative, and choose the simplest structure whose guarantees match those needs.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because List the dominant operations and invariants first, compare at least one alternative, and choose the simplest structure whose guarantees match those needs.

examples.title: Data-structure choice: boundary case
examples.situation: A permission check needs unique role membership, while a profile lookup needs a map from user id to profile record.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A permission check needs unique role membership, while a profile lookup needs a map from user id to profile record.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Big-O labels alone omit constant costs, scale, mutation patterns, library guarantees, concurrency, persistence, and readability for the actual workload.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: One structure, such as a list, is always best because it can represent every small dataset somehow.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Defend a data-structure choice and identify a reasonable alternative's trade-off.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: One structure, such as a list, is always best because it can represent every small dataset somehow.
misconceptions.correction: List the dominant operations and invariants first, compare at least one alternative, and choose the simplest structure whose guarantees match those needs.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: One structure, such as a list, is always best because it can represent every small dataset somehow.

analogy.example: An organized storeroom can model Data-structure choice: shelves preserve order, labeled bins group fields, membership lists avoid duplicates, and indexes locate values.
usefulFor: Comparing organization by order, name, membership, lookup, and update needs
limitations: Physical storage hides concrete complexity, equality, hashing, mutation, allocation, and concurrency guarantees.
Big-O labels alone omit constant costs, scale, mutation patterns, library guarantees, concurrency, persistence, and readability for the actual workload.

recap.summary: You should now be able to defend a data-structure choice and identify a reasonable alternative's trade-off. Keep this boundary in view: Big-O labels alone omit constant costs, scale, mutation patterns, library guarantees, concurrency, persistence, and readability for the actual workload.

Apply the model to this case from memory: A permission check needs unique role membership, while a profile lookup needs a map from user id to profile record.
